title: Maamoul mad
subtitle: A baked semolina and date tray pastry from Lebanon
excerpt: Maamoul mad is the tray-baked version of the Lebanese pressed-mould biscuit maamoul. A thick semolina dough is pressed into a tin, a layer of date paste is spread over it, then a second layer of dough covers the top. It is baked, then cut into diamonds or squares. Simpler to make than individual maamoul and equally fragrant with rose water.
sourceNotes: Maamoul mad is a Syrian and Lebanese tray-baked variation of the traditional maamoul biscuit, offering the same rose water and date flavours without the labour-intensive individual mould-pressing step. The dish appears in Levantine cookbooks and is commonly made for Eid celebrations. This recipe is synthesised from the established Syrian and Lebanese baking tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cut into pieces and freeze in an airtight container for up to 2 months. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps well for up to a week at room temperature in an airtight tin. Improves after a day as the rose water scent deepens.

baking.decoratingTechnique: fork-tined top layer, icing sugar dusted

glossaryTerms.definition: Maamoul mad uses a dough made from semolina and plain flour combined with melted butter, rose water and a little water. Semolina gives the pastry its characteristic slightly gritty, crumbly texture that softens as it rests. The dough needs at least an hour of resting after mixing so the semolina can absorb the liquid fully; rushed dough crumbles when pressed into the tin.

content.text: must rest before it is used; mixing and immediately pressing it will give a crumbly, sandy result. Make the dough, cover, and leave for at least an hour. The date filling can be prepared during this time.
Make the dough (at least 1 hour before baking)

Combine the 300 g semolina, 150 g plain flour, 60 g icing sugar and quarter teaspoon salt in a large bowl. Pour in the 150 g melted butter and rub together until the mixture resembles damp sand. Add the 2 tbsp rose water and 1 tbsp orange blossom water and mix. Add the 3 tbsp milk a little at a time, working the dough until it just comes together into a soft, pliable mass. Cover and leave to rest for 1 hour at room temperature.

Blend the 300 g stoned dates with 20 g butter and half a teaspoon cinnamon in a food processor, or mash together in a bowl, until a smooth paste forms. If the dates are very dry, warm them briefly in the microwave first.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C. Grease and line a 20 x 20 cm square tin. Divide the dough in half. Press one half evenly over the base of the tin, using wet fingers to smooth it flat. Spread the date paste over the dough in an even layer. Flatten the second half of dough between your palms into a sheet and lay it over the date filling, pressing down gently to cover. Use a fork to press a pattern across the top. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the surface is a light golden brown throughout and feels set. Leave to cool completely in the tin, then dust with icing sugar and cut into diamond or square shapes.

items.problem: Top layer crumbled when pressing it over the filling
items.solution: The dough was too dry. It should be pliable, not crumbly. Add water a teaspoon at a time until it holds together. Alternatively, flatten the dough between two sheets of baking paper with a rolling pin and carefully flip it onto the filling.

They were cut before the pastry was completely cold. Maamoul mad must be left to cool entirely in the tin before cutting; warm pastry is very fragile. Use a sharp knife and cut with one firm downward motion, not a sawing action.
